1: When Ish-bosheth son of Saul heard that Abner had died at Hebron, his courage failed and all Israel was dismayed. Ish-bosheth (ʾîš-bōšeṯ, man of shame) was likely named Esh-baal (man of Baal) at birth — 1 Chronicles 8:33 preserves the original form — and had the theophoric element replaced with bōšeṯ (shame) by later scribal convention, as happened with several Saulide names. He ruled the northern tribes from Mahanaim in Transjordan for two years (2 Samuel 2:10), sustained politically by Abner's military support. With Abner dead (2 Samuel 3:27), the political infrastructure of the rival kingdom has collapsed.
2: Saul's son had two men who were commanders of raiding bands (śārê gəḏûḏîm): Baanah and Rechab, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, from the tribe of Benjamin. The gəḏûḏ (raiding party/band) was a tactical unit used for swift strikes — distinct from the main army. These two men hold a rank that gave them access to the royal quarters without arousing suspicion.
3: 'The Beerothites fled to Gittaim and are sojourners there to this day.' Beeroth was one of the four Hivite-Gibeonite cities that deceived Israel into a treaty in Joshua 9:17. The flight to Gittaim is unexplained in the narrative but likely reflects the aftermath of Saul's attack on the Gibeonites (referenced in 2 Samuel 21:2) — the Beerothites, as Gibeonite confederates, fled when Saul's aggression began. Their status as gērîm (resident aliens) in Gittaim connects forward to the Gibeonite reparations of 2 Samuel 21.
4: A parenthetical note: Jonathan's son Mephibosheth was five years old when the news of Saul and Jonathan came from Jezreel. His nurse took him and fled; in her hurry he fell and became lame in both feet. This parenthesis prepares for David's treatment of Mephibosheth in 2 Samuel 9. The note is placed here — in the middle of the Ish-bosheth narrative — to remind the reader of the last surviving claimant to the Saulide throne and his disability. The information that he fell while fleeing frames his lameness as a consequence of the same crisis (Saul's death) that eliminated the rest of his family.
5: Baanah and Rechab set out and came to the house of Ish-bosheth at the heat of the day, while he was taking his midday rest. The 'heat of the day' is the siesta period in which the household would be less guarded — the same temporal setting as David's fateful rooftop encounter in 2 Samuel 11:2. The narrative's detail about Ish-bosheth taking his rest emphasizes his vulnerability and the opportunistic timing of the assassination.
6: They came into the middle of the house as if to get wheat, and they stabbed him in the stomach. The pretext — fetching wheat from the house's store — gave them access to the interior without armed escort. The quick, decisive nature of the attack (wayyakkūhû ʾel-haḥōmeš, they struck him in the belly) ensured Ish-bosheth had no opportunity to resist or call for help.
7: They came into the house while he was lying on his bed in his bedroom and struck him and put him to death and beheaded him. They took his head and went by the way of the Arabah all night. The Arabah route — the Jordan Valley running south from the Sea of Galilee to the Dead Sea — was the direct road from Mahanaim to Hebron, bypassing the central highlands where they might be recognized. They traveled by night to avoid detection, bringing the head as physical proof of their deed for David.
8: They brought the head of Ish-bosheth to David at Hebron and said to the king: Here is the head of Ish-bosheth, the son of Saul, your enemy, who sought your life. YHWH has avenged my lord the king this day on Saul and his offspring. The killers frame their act as divinely executed justice — the same justification the Amalekite used in 2 Samuel 1:10-16 when he claimed to have killed Saul. Both attempts to curry David's favor by producing the death of a Saulide backfired severely.
9: David answered Rechab and Baanah his brother, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite: As YHWH lives, who has redeemed my life from every adversity. David's response begins with a divine oath — ḥay YHWH ʾăšer pāḏāh ʾeṯ-napšî mikol-ṣārāh — and immediately establishes that YHWH, not human action, is the agent of his survival. The term pāḏâ (redeemed/ransomed) is the commercial-legal term for buying someone out of obligation — David's life has been protected by YHWH's own action, not by the murders these men have committed.
10: When one told me, Saul is dead, thinking he was bringing good news, I seized him and killed him at Ziklag — that was the reward I gave him for his news. David cites the precedent of 2 Samuel 1:1-16: he executed the Amalekite messenger who claimed to have killed Saul. The principle is now applied retroactively to establish what these men should have expected. The word bəśōrāh (good news) is used ironically — what the messenger thought was news that would earn him a reward was instead the ground of his execution.
11: How much more, when wicked men have killed a righteous man in his own house on his own bed, shall I not now require his blood at your hand and destroy you from the earth? David's legal reasoning is explicit: if executing the messenger who reported Saul's death was just (even though Saul had been David's persecutor), executing the murderers of Ish-bosheth (ṣaddîq, righteous/innocent) is even more clearly required. The ṣaddîq designation for Ish-bosheth does not mean morally superior to David but legally innocent of any offense against David.
12: David commanded his young men, and they killed them and cut off their hands and their feet and hanged them beside the pool at Hebron. But they took the head of Ish-bosheth and buried it in the tomb of Abner at Hebron. The mutilation — cutting off hands and feet — is a form of public execution and display that denies the offenders a proper burial while communicating the king's condemnation. The pool at Hebron was a public location; the displayed bodies served as a warning. The burial of Ish-bosheth's head in Abner's tomb is an act of respect that reconnects the murdered son of Saul with his general — both are given honorable burial under David's authority.
